patches.find: (iv) PRED-TIER-3 — the dome's headline forward-looking prediction for the August 2026 total solar eclipse — derives its 10–13 µGal SG-pulse target by dividing the predicted ΔB at named stations by κ. Without κ as a real coupling, PRED-TIER-3 has no published mechanism producing the 10–13 µGal target. The dome cannot drop κ without dropping its strongest live discriminator.
patches.replace: (iv) PRED-TIER-3 — the dome's headline forward-looking prediction for the August 2026 total solar eclipse — publishes its 10–13 µGal SG-pulse target without an explicit derivation in predictions.html or in the dome’s monitor.py source. The natural reading is that 10–13 µGal = (predicted ΔB at named stations) / κ, and we have located no alternative derivation in the dome's published material. Absent such an alternative, κ owns the derivation. The dome cannot drop κ without dropping its strongest live discriminator — or, alternatively, publishing in advance of August 2026 a non-κ derivation for the 10–13 µGal target.
patches.rationale: ISS-1975 (H7) + ISS-1986 (H12 coupled): reframe PRED-TIER-3 derivation as inference (natural reading + no alternative located), add source pointers, close Chebychev-derivation escape surface.

patches.find: continuously sampled across C1 and C4, and reported no eclipse-related gravity signal above the instrument noise floor of order 0.01–0.1 µGal at minute timescales.
patches.replace: continuously sampled across C1 and C4, and reported no eclipse-related gravity signal above the instrument noise floor of order 0.01–0.1 µGal at minute timescales. The Mansinha 2001 reduction pipeline applies standard tidal-model and atmospheric-pressure-admittance corrections at sub-daily timescales but retains minute-resolution residuals across the C1–C4 interval — the band where a κ-predicted contact-phase pulse of a few µGal would appear. The published null therefore reflects the absence of a signal at minute-scale temporal resolution after standard corrections, not a band-pass filter that would mathematically exclude the predicted transient. If the dome argues κ predicts a sub-minute pulse narrower than that band, the methodological caveat is addressed in What the dome could do below.
patches.rationale: ISS-1974 (H6): characterize Mansinha 2001 pipeline, confirm minute-resolution residuals retained, address Chebychev-low-pass rebuttal on its own grounds.

patches.find: The 2009 East Asian campaign, comprising approximately eight gravimeters across six Chinese sites with continuous sampling spanning C1 through C4, reported no eclipse-coincident gravity perturbation; this is the very null the dome itself cites as WIN-014, attributed to "Sun et al. 2010" (the closest verified peer-reviewed Chinese-language record being doi:10.6038/cjg20130306, 2013).
patches.replace: The 2009 East Asian campaign, comprising approximately eight gravimeters across six Chinese sites with continuous sampling spanning C1 through C4, reported no eclipse-coincident gravity perturbation in the published minute-binned Level-3 residuals; this is the very null the dome itself cites as WIN-014, attributed to "Sun et al. 2010" (the closest verified peer-reviewed Chinese-language record being doi:10.6038/cjg20130306, 2013). Both the 1999 (Mansinha 2001) and the published Chinese 2009 residuals report SG noise at minute timescales — the standard Level-3 binning — so a κ-predicted pulse of width one minute or longer would appear in these residuals. A sub-minute transient (e.g., a 30-second contact-phase spike) would be amplitude-diluted by roughly a factor of two in the one-minute bin, reducing a predicted 3–9 µGal pulse to 1.5–4.5 µGal observed — still well above the 0.1–1 µGal post-reduction residual floor and 1–2 orders of magnitude above the 0.01–0.1 µGal raw instrumental floor.
patches.rationale: ISS-1987 (H13): specify temporal resolution of published SG residuals as minute-binned Level-3, add binning-dilution arithmetic.

patches.find: the suggestion that the coupling mechanism changed between 1999 and 2026 because of a parameter shift the dome's own monitor.py does not validate) read as post-hoc rescues constructed after the data refused to cooperate. This is the same methodological gap the OTS canary identifies for the dome more generally
patches.replace:
the suggestion that the coupling mechanism changed between 1999 and 2026 because of a parameter shift the dome's own monitor.py does not validate) read as post-hoc rescues constructed after the data refused to cooperate.

One of those four carve-outs — the bedrock argument — has a published-prior anchor that the others lack: the dome labels κ the “Bedrock Induction Ratio” in WIN-058b’s full claim text (verified in data/wins.json WIN-058b.claim). The naming could be read as a bedrock-conditional scope hint, present before the SG-null data became inconvenient. But the published-prior naming does not, on its own, rescue the vise. The dome publishes no quantitative bedrock conductivity threshold above or below which the coupling applies, no continent-or-substrate gating rule, and no observation-screening criterion for which SG sites count. The constant κ = 1.677 is applied identically across the live-power dashboard, WIN-012/-013/-014, and PRED-TIER-3 — no station-by-station bedrock classification. Treating κ as bedrock-conditional retroactively to exempt 1999 (Membach) and 2009 (the Chinese SG sites) requires the dome to publish, in advance of August 2026, the specific bedrock conductivity values and the station-by-station bedrock classifications that gate the coupling, and to commit that the August 2026 SG sites fall on the “coupling-on” side. The Chebychev-pipeline, instrumental-noise, and parameter-shift rescues have no comparable published-prior anchor and remain post-hoc by construction.

This is the same methodological gap the OTS canary identifies for the dome more generally
patches.rationale: ISS-1984 (H10): acknowledge Bedrock Induction Ratio published-prior anchor, show naming alone insufficient without quantitative threshold, specify what dome must publish to make bedrock-conditionality falsifiable.

patches.find: WIN-068 (39-eclipse magnetic ensemble 1991–2016, Std Model Explains — research candidate for ensemble-scale κ refutation, 207 magnetic measurements, each implying a co-located SG prediction through κ)
patches.replace: WIN-068 (39-eclipse magnetic ensemble 1991–2016, Std Model Explains — 207 magnetic measurements at INTERMAGNET observatories; the SG-prediction subset is the eclipses where a superconducting gravimeter was continuously sampling near totality, plausibly 5–10 events when intersecting the WIN-068 path catalogue with the Global Geodynamics Project SG site/operational-date archive — each such co-located case implies an additional κ-refutation test)
patches.rationale: ISS-1985 (H11): correct overstated WIN-068 scope from 207-each-SG to accurate INTERMAGNET-magnetic scope with plausible 5-10 SG-co-located subset.

patches.find: A few-µGal predicted pulse exceeds the SG noise floor by factors of 30–600. If κ predicts that pulse, the SGs that were sampling those eclipses would have recorded it. They did not.
patches.replace: Using Mansinha 2001’s instrumental noise floor of 0.01–0.1 µGal, the predicted 3–9 µGal pulse exceeds noise by factors of 30–900; using the Crossley 2013 and Hinderer 2022 post-reduction residual floor of 0.1–1 µGal, the margin narrows to 3–90×. Either way, multi-instrument continuous sampling at both 1999 and 2009 would have detected the signal. If κ predicts that pulse, the SGs that were sampling those eclipses would have recorded it. They did not.
patches.rationale: ISS-1977 (H9): split 30-600x into two explicit noise-floor bands (30-900x raw, 3-90x post-reduction).
